common_name: Bella Rosa Tomato (Disease-Resistant Gulf South Hybrid)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 start in January for March transplanting to harvest in May–June. Bella Rosa is a determinate hybrid recommended specifically for Southern gardens where diseases and heat are primary challenges.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Bella Rosa is a determinate hybrid tomato developed for disease resistance and heat adaptation in humid Southern gardens, an AAS (All-America Selections) winner that earned recognition for reliable production in difficult growing conditions. The medium-large (8–12 oz), round, firm, bright red fruits have excellent flavor — more complex and acidic than purely mild hybrids but not harsh — with thick walls and small seed cavity that make them suitable for slicing, sandwiches, and fresh preparations. The broad disease resistance package (Verticillium wilt, Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2, root-knot nematodes, tobacco mosaic virus, and gray leaf spot) addresses the main failure modes of standard tomatoes in humid Gulf South gardens where disease pressure is severe. The determinate compact plants (3–4 feet) produce a concentrated crop over 3–4 weeks, ideal for gardeners who want a concentrated harvest for preserving, sauce-making, or sharing with neighbors. An excellent choice for zone 9 spring and fall tomato seasons where disease resistance is more important than variety novelty.
sources: All-America Selections — Bella Rosa AAS winner
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production Guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tomato Variety Selection

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 Florida 47 can be grown as a spring crop (transplant February–March for May harvest) or fall crop (transplant August for October–November harvest). Florida 47 is a determinate type well-adapted to the Florida and Gulf Coast growing windows.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Florida 47 is a University of Florida-developed determinate hybrid tomato bred specifically for commercial and home production in the hot, humid Florida and Gulf Coast environment — part of a long series of Florida-bred tomatoes (including Florida 91, Floradade, and Solar Set) developed by UF's tomato breeding program to address the unique challenges of Southern tomato production. The large (8–12 oz), globe to slightly flattened, firm, bright red fruits have the thick walls, uniform shape, and long shelf life that commercial producers require, but perform equally well for home gardens; the flavor is balanced and pleasant without the blandness of many commercial types. Excellent disease resistance including Fusarium wilt races 1, 2, and 3, Verticillium wilt, gray leaf spot, and tobacco mosaic virus — the full-package resistance crucial for reliable performance in disease-prone Southern soils. Florida 47 is widely planted commercially in the Immokalee, Florida production region during the fall and spring seasons and performs similarly well for home gardeners across zone 8b–11.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Gulf Coast Research and Education Center — Tomato Breeding Program
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Commercial Tomato Production Guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Vegetable Variety Evaluations

common_name: Plum Regal Tomato (Disease-Resistant Plum Paste Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Plum Regal is a determinate paste-type tomato that sets a concentrated crop over 3–4 weeks. In zone 9, aim for spring harvest in May–June by transplanting in early March.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Plum Regal is a determinate plum-type paste tomato developed by Cornell University's tomato breeding program specifically to improve on the disease susceptibility of heirloom Roma and similar paste types while maintaining the thick walls, dense flesh, and low moisture content that make plum tomatoes superior for sauce-making and processing. Plum Regal carries resistance to late blight (Phytophthora infestans) — the disease that wiped out heirloom paste tomatoes across much of the Northeast during the late blight epidemic of 2009 — as well as Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2 and Verticillium wilt. The medium-large (3–5 oz), deep red, oval fruits have very thick walls, small seed cavities, and excellent sauce-making characteristics with rich, concentrated tomato flavor. An outstanding choice for humid-climate paste tomato production, for home canners who want reliable crops without the disease susceptibility of traditional paste heirlooms, and for any gardener growing tomatoes where late blight or Fusarium are recurring problems.
sources: Cornell University vegetable breeding program publications
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Plum Regal variety notes

common_name: Mountain Fresh Plus Tomato (Southern Disease-Resistant Slicer)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Mountain Fresh Plus is a determinate hybrid well-suited to Southern spring and fall growing windows. In zone 9, transplant February–March for spring harvest and August for fall harvest.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Mountain Fresh Plus is a North Carolina State University-bred determinate hybrid tomato from the Mountain series (which also includes Mountain Merit, Mountain Spring, and Mountain Pride), developed to combine excellent fruit quality with broad disease resistance for Southern and transitional-climate production. The large (8–12 oz), globe, bright red fruits have firm, crack-resistant skin, good internal color, and balanced sweet-acid flavor — a reliable all-purpose slicing tomato without the flavor complexity of heirlooms but with vastly superior disease resistance and shelf life. Disease resistance includes Fusarium wilt races 1, 2, and 3, Verticillium wilt, gray leaf spot, tomato spotted wilt virus, and root-knot nematodes (races 0, 1, 2, 3) — one of the broadest resistance packages available in a Southern-bred tomato. An excellent choice for zone 8–10 home gardens and market growers who need reliable, marketable tomatoes with broad disease protection in humid, challenging Southern conditions.
sources: North Carolina State University Mountain Horticultural Crops Research and Extension Center
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Variety Evaluation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Vegetable Variety Evaluations

common_name: Fortex Pole Bean (Extra-Long French Filet Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; Fortex is a vigorous climbing pole bean reaching 7–9 feet — provide a sturdy trellis, pole tepee, or strong fence for support. Harvest pods frequently and while still young (10–11 inches long) before seeds begin to bulge, which keeps the plants productive and maintains pod tenderness.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 60°F; provide a sturdy trellis or support structure before planting and harvest regularly while pods are still slender and young. Fortex is considered by many gardeners and chefs to be the finest French filet (haricot vert) style pole bean available — a European heirloom that produces extraordinarily long (10–12 inch), very slender, round-podded, deep green beans with exceptional tenderness and refined flavor that places it far above standard snap beans for fresh eating and French-style preparations. Unlike flat, wide snap beans, Fortex pods are round, very slender (filet-style), and remain tender and flavorful well beyond the size at which most beans become tough — they can be harvested at any point from finger-thin to full pencil thickness and maintain excellent quality. The flavor is genuinely superior, with a rich green bean taste and very fine texture that makes Fortex the preferred bean for blanching, sautéing with garlic and butter, and all preparations where bean quality is the focus. Very productive vines climb 7–9 feet and bear continuously when picked frequently. An outstanding bean for serious vegetable gardeners and those who want restaurant-quality produce from their home garden.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Fortex pole bean variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Snap Bean Variety Evaluation
